source: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification

input_row.title: Prefer using secrets as files over secrets as environment variables
input_row.description: rewrite application code to read secrets from mounted secret files
input_row.audit: Kubernetes supports mounting secrets as data volumes or as environment variables. Minimize the use of environment variable secrets.
input_row.remediation: It is reasonably common for application code to log out its environment (particularly in the event of an error). This will include any secret values passed in as environment variables, so secrets can easily be exposed to any user or entity who has access to the logs. Impact: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
input_row.impact: Run the following command to find references to objects which use environment variables defined from secrets. kubectl get all -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@..secretKeyRef)]} {.kind} {.metadata.name} {"\n"}{end}' -A
input_row.reference: If possible, rewrite application code to read secrets from mounted secret files, rather than from environment variables. Default Value: By default, secrets are not defined References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/security/benchmark/azure/security-controls-v2- identity-management#im-7-eliminate-unintended-credential-exposure Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod
input_row.source: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
null: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/secret/#using-secrets 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/security/benchmark/azure/security-controls-v2- identity-management#im-7-eliminate-unintended-credential-exposure Additional Information: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod

gpt_response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:** 
- The control can be verified using Kubernetes APIs. Though it involves secrets and their management, the audit command provided can be run through CLI, which indicates it can be integrated into an automated compliance tool.

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_aks_secrets_environment_variables_verified

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
- The automated program will check Kubernetes configurations where secrets are used as environment variables instead of as mounted files.
- **Azure Resource Manager APIs or Azure CLI commands to use:** The command using `kubectl` can be executed in the context of the Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS).
- **Specific resources to enumerate:** 
  - All Kubernetes pods and deployments across clusters within the AKS implementation.
- **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:**
  - Verify if any Kubernetes resources are using `secretKeyRef` to extract secrets as environment variables.
- **Clear Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:**
  - Pass: No Kubernetes deployments or pods are using secrets defined as environment variables.
  - Fail: At least one deployment or pod is utilizing secrets via environment variables.
- **Example API endpoint or CLI command:**
  ```bash
  kubectl get all -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@..secretKeyRef)]} {.kind} {.metadata.name} {"\n"}{end}' -A
  ```
  This command retrieves all Kubernetes resources and examines whether they reference any secrets through environment variable declarations.
